other_observers: Sally and Danny Paez of Albuquerque, NM; First known detection
specific_location: Along bank of Animas River 1/2 mile upstream from River Road Bridge in south Durango.
description: A very small passerine with a thin straight bill, a round body, and a very short stub tail which was usually held in an erect posture. The bird was a warm brown above with some pale barring on the scapulars, nearly-white spots along the leading edge of each wing, and darker barring on the tail. The bird was overall paler below from throat to belly with some indistinct darker barring. As the breast blended into the abdomen the underparts became darker, now with paler barring concentrated on the flanks. The face showed pale supercilliaries, extending mostly behind the eyes, and a bit of spotting or barring on the auricular region. The overall structure and plumage was distinctly wren-like and very small size, very short tail, nearly-straight bill, and distinct supercillia rule out all but the Winter/Pacific complex.Behaviors: Very secretive and difficult to see. The bird was calling occasionally when I first detected it. After declaring that I thought it was a winter wren, based only on vocalizations, I caught a few brief glimpses and then the bird disappeared and went silent. Danny Paez softly played <10 seconds of Winter Wren song on an iPhone and the bird almost immediately responded by calling back. The vocalizations escalated to the "rachet" calls and the bird slowly worked in nearly a complete circle around us-the source of the playback. It would work through dense tangles of brush and vines, occasionally showing itself for a few seconds before flying in an arc to the next pile of brush. It would move through the brush in a mouse-like manner and would rarely maintain an exposed position for more than a couple of seconds. It kept it's stubby tail cocked at almost all times and often bobbed and pivoted it's entire body.Call: Very distinctive calls, "chmp" and "chmp-chmp" with a quality VERY similar to song sparrow. I called out Song Sparrow after the first note, but then noted it was slightly off and delivered rapidly and often in pairs, though sometimes individual "chmps". Following playback the bird also delivered some very rapid machine-gun "rachet" series of stacatto call notes.Plumage: Unknown
